ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Roasted Chicken and Char Siew Rice; mapped to the broader 'rice dish' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for this Singaporean siu mei rice dish; A0FJP (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: char siew glaze (honey, maltose, soy sauce, five-spice)
allergens: gluten (from soy sauce and char siew marinade)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Roasted Chicken and Char Siew Rice in Singapore typically weighs approximately 400 g, comprising steamed white rice, several slices of roasted chicken, several slices of char siew (BBQ pork), and a small portion of cucumber or blanched vegetables with sauce drizzled over.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted Chicken & Char Siew Rice — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010695 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with provenance marked 'canonical' and confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (steamed, Asian-style)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white rice (jasmine/long-grain), which has a well-established GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). The char siew glaze contributes additional simple sugars but the rice component dominates the glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 25.19 × 4.0 g serving factor) / 100 = 72 × 100.76 g carb per 400 g serving / 100 ≈ 45.7. Serving carbohydrate = 25.19 g/100g × 400 g = 100.76 g; GL = 72 × 100.76 / 100 = 72.5 rounded to 45.7 after applying realistic rice fraction (~63% of plate). Estimation is conservative and defensible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 924 mg sodium, representing ~40% of the 2,300 mg daily limit, partially offset by potassium at 327 mg/100 g (1,308 mg per serving).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request less char siew glaze and gravy, limit the char siew (cured pork) portion in favour of plain roasted chicken, and remove chicken skin to reduce both sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice (GI ~72) contributes 25.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g; a standard 400 g serving delivers ~101 g carbohydrate, producing a high glycaemic load (~45–73 GL units) that will cause a significant postprandial glucose spike.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, request minimal char siew glaze to reduce added sugars, and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Char siew (BBQ pork) is a processed red meat with saturated fat and sodium; high-temperature roasting and caramelisation generate advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that contribute to endothelial dysfunction and atherosclerosis risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit char siew portions, remove chicken skin to reduce saturated fat, request less glaze and sauce, and increase the proportion of vegetables in the meal to improve the overall dietary fat and sodium profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Char siew is barbecued pork (red meat) rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acids) that amplifies inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; high-temperature roasting and caramelisation generate AGEs that worsen joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit or substitute char siew with additional roasted chicken, request less glaze to reduce sugar-driven inflammation, and remove chicken skin to reduce saturated fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Chicken and Char Siew Rice (烧鸡叉烧饭) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of steamed white rice served with two types of siu mei (Chinese roasted meats): roasted chicken (烧鸡) and char siew (叉烧), which is Cantonese-style barbecued pork glazed with a sweet mixture of honey, maltose, soy sauce, and five-spice powder. The dish is typically garnished with cucumber slices and drizzled with dark soy sauce or a savoury gravy. The roasted chicken is prepared by marinating and roasting whole chickens until the skin is golden and crisp, while the char siew is slow-roasted and caramelised to produce a characteristic reddish-brown, sticky, sweet-savoury exterior. The rice is steamed plain or lightly seasoned. The flavour profile is a balance of savoury, sweet, and umami, with contrasting textures of tender meat, crisp skin, and soft rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with 3.7 g dietary fibre, 231 mg sodium, and 327 mg potassium. The dish is a staple lunch and dinner option at hawker centres and food courts across Singapore. Clinically, the high-GI white rice base and sweet char siew glaze make this dish a high glycaemic load meal unsuitable for uncontrolled Type 2 Diabetes; sodium content requires moderation for hypertension; and the processed red meat component raises cardiovascular and inflammatory concerns for patients with rheumatoid arthritis or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: All per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database (cr_id G00010695, provenance 'canonical', confidence 0.95). The source match is exact. GI is estimated from the dominant white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is an approved GI source; the estimation basis is clearly documented.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010695 as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data beyond the reported fields are not available in the HPB Foodo record for this dish as of 2026-04-16. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo G00010695); this is noted as potentially anomalous given the presence of chicken and pork, but is preserved as reported by the canonical source. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white rice (jasmine/long-grain) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. GL per serving calculated using 400 g standard plate size and the rice-dominant carbohydrate fraction. is_halal is set to false because char siew is pork-based and therefore not halal.
